key_insight: High-entropy disorder engineering in Fe2VAl full-Heusler alloys lowers lattice thermal conductivity and raises thermoelectric performance, enabling practical module-level gains.
engineering_relevance: The work shows that low-cost, non-toxic, mechanically workable Fe2VAl-based materials can be scaled into robust thermoelectric modules with strong output over 300–673 K.
method: Heavy-element doping plus controlled off-stoichiometry to create substitutional disorder on all lattice sites
Hot-pressed scaled-up material batches to check reproducibility against lab-scale samples
Direct brazing of TE legs onto copper electrodes to build a 6×6-leg module
evidence_signal: The optimized alloys reach κL ≈ 2.3 W m−1 K−1 and zTave ≈ 0.3 from 300–500 K, and the assembled module shows high output power and one of the highest reported conversion efficiencies for Fe2VAl-based generators.
watch_item: Performance is sensitive to processing, since scaled-up batches showed slightly higher resistivity without post-annealing, which may affect module efficiency.
paper_title: High-entropy Fe$_2$VAl-based thermoelectric modules with improved conversion efficiency
